ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a notable new application of joint cross-lingual parameter tying in an encoder–decoder gated RNN with attention for morphological paradigm completion, enabling zero-shot and one-shot transfer which is not standard in prior work. It is empirically validated across 21 language pairs with up to 58% accuracy gains and includes careful ciphering experiments to separate true linguistic transfer from regularization effects. The approach clearly differentiates itself from prior monolingual/no-transfer baselines and extends existing neural sequence models in a non-trivial, task-specific way, though it builds on established encoder–decoder architectures rather than introducing entirely new model classes. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new application of joint multi-task cross-lingual training—tying parameters across a high-resource and low-resource language in an encoder–decoder gated RNN with attention and explicit language/morphological tags—which enables zero- and one-shot morphological paradigm completion. The experimental results (up to 58% accuracy gains across 21 language pairs) and analysis of language relatedness provide substantive empirical novelty within the paradigm-completion setting. While the method builds on existing seq2seq and transfer-learning techniques, adapting them for one-shot cross-lingual morphological transfer is a non-trivial and well-differentiated contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a notable new application of joint cross-lingual parameter tying in an encoder–decoder gated RNN with attention for morphological paradigm completion, enabling zero-shot and one-shot transfer which is not standard in prior work. It is empirically validated across 21 language pairs with up to 58% accuracy gains and includes careful ciphering experiments to separate true linguistic transfer from regularization effects. The approach clearly differentiates itself from prior monolingual/no-transfer baselines and extends existing neural sequence models in a non-trivial, task-specific way, though it builds on established encoder–decoder architectures rather than introducing entirely new model classes.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new application of joint multi-task cross-lingual training—tying parameters across a high-resource and low-resource language in an encoder–decoder gated RNN with attention and explicit language/morphological tags—which enables zero- and one-shot morphological paradigm completion. The experimental results (up to 58% accuracy gains across 21 language pairs) and analysis of language relatedness provide substantive empirical novelty within the paradigm-completion setting. While the method builds on existing seq2seq and transfer-learning techniques, adapting them for one-shot cross-lingual morphological transfer is a non-trivial and well-differentiated contribution.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are generally supported: a joint encoder–decoder multi-task transfer method is evaluated on 21 language pairs (SIGMORPHON 2016, Portuguese–Spanish, Arabic–Spanish) and shows large accuracy gains (up to 58%) with appropriate baselines (no-transfer) and diagnostic ciphering experiments that distinguish true linguistic transfer from regularization effects. There are no complex mathematical derivations to vet and the model specification and hyperparameters are reported. Minor concerns remain about the experimental reporting (no mention of statistical significance testing and an unclear description of some baselines), but these do not overturn the empirical evidence or the paper’s core conclusions about the importance of language relatedness for transfer. | B(4): The main claims (cross-lingual encoder–decoder transfer improves low-resource paradigm completion and enables zero-/one-shot learning) are supported by multi-language experiments (21 language pairs) and architecture/method details (joint multi-task attention RNN, language-tagged inputs). The authors also include useful controls (ciphering experiments) that distinguish true linguistic transfer from mere regularization, and they acknowledge limitations (transfer mainly among related languages and regularization sensitivity). Minor technical issues remain: the summary does not report statistical significance testing and the description of baselines is slightly unclear, but these do not undermine the primary experimental support.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are generally supported: a joint encoder–decoder multi-task transfer method is evaluated on 21 language pairs (SIGMORPHON 2016, Portuguese–Spanish, Arabic–Spanish) and shows large accuracy gains (up to 58%) with appropriate baselines (no-transfer) and diagnostic ciphering experiments that distinguish true linguistic transfer from regularization effects. There are no complex mathematical derivations to vet and the model specification and hyperparameters are reported. Minor concerns remain about the experimental reporting (no mention of statistical significance testing and an unclear description of some baselines), but these do not overturn the empirical evidence or the paper’s core conclusions about the importance of language relatedness for transfer.

The authors also include useful controls (ciphering experiments) that distinguish true linguistic transfer from mere regularization, and they acknowledge limitations (transfer mainly among related languages and regularization sensitivity). CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The summary indicates a well-organized paper with a logical flow (method, experiments, conclusion) and that the main contributions are stated clearly early on (novel cross-lingual transfer method, one-shot/zero-shot claims). Notation and model description appear consistent and well-defined (encoder–decoder gated RNN with attention, input parameterization), and writing quality is reported as good. One minor reservation is that the summary does not mention the clarity/effectiveness of figures, tables, or examples, and the paper notes some presentation challenges around regularization details in low-resource settings, suggesting small presentation improvements could be made. | B(4): The paper is well organized and presents the method, datasets, baselines, and experimental results in a logical order (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion), and the main contributions are stated early (novel cross-lingual encoder–decoder transfer for paradigm completion). Notation and model description are clear and concrete (encoder–decoder gated RNN with attention, inputs as language tag + morphological tag + lemma characters), and hyperparameters and evaluation metrics are reported. Figures/tables and examples appear informative given the broad experimental coverage (21 language pairs, multiple families) and the thorough related-work discussion; overall writing quality is good, with only minor presentation issues preventing a top score.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work addresses a core NLP problem (cross-lingual morphological paradigm completion) using standard, state-of-the-art methods (encoder-decoder gated RNNs with attention) and datasets (SIGMORPHON 2016, experiments on 21 language pairs including Portuguese–Spanish and Arabic–Spanish). It evaluates against appropriate baselines (no-transfer, language baselines), reports hyperparameters, and uses accuracy measures typical for the venue. The one-shot/zero-shot framing and analysis of language relatedness make it directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences concerned with low-resource and transfer learning. Overall it is well positioned and methodologically aligned with expectations for such conferences. | B(5): This paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses cross-lingual transfer for morphological paradigm completion (a core NLP problem) using modern neural encoder–decoder models with attention and evaluates on SIGMORPHON 2016 and 21 language pairs (e.g., Portuguese–Spanish, Arabic–Spanish). The methodology, baselines (including no-transfer), accuracy-based evaluation, and reporting of hyperparameters match community standards, and the one-shot/zero-shot transfer claims are of clear interest to both NLP and ML audiences. Limitations are acknowledged (transfer mainly among related languages; regularization issues) and related work is covered thoroughly, indicating correct positioning for the venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This work addresses a core NLP problem (cross-lingual morphological paradigm completion) using standard, state-of-the-art methods (encoder-decoder gated RNNs with attention) and datasets (SIGMORPHON 2016, experiments on 21 language pairs including Portuguese–Spanish and Arabic–Spanish). It evaluates against appropriate baselines (no-transfer, language baselines), reports hyperparameters, and uses accuracy measures typical for the venue. The one-shot/zero-shot framing and analysis of language relatedness make it directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences concerned with low-resource and transfer learning. Overall it is well positioned and methodologically aligned with expectations for such conferences.

The methodology, baselines (including no-transfer), accuracy-based evaluation, and reporting of hyperparameters match community standards, and the one-shot/zero-shot transfer claims are of clear interest to both NLP and ML audiences. IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—paradigm completion for low-resource languages—by demonstrating one-shot and zero-shot neural cross-lingual transfer and reporting up to 58% accuracy gains using labeled data from high-resource languages. Experimental breadth (21 language pairs across four families, SIGMORPHON 2016 and language-specific experiments) and the clear finding that relatedness strongly conditions transfer make this likely to change how researchers approach low-resource morphology. The limitation that transfer is mainly effective among related languages (and that some gains from unrelated sources may be regularization artifacts) prevents a fully transformative claim, but the work nonetheless opens significant, widely useful research directions for cross-lingual morphology and low-resource NLP. | B(4): The paper tackles an important low-resource NLP problem by proposing a one-shot/zero-shot neural cross-lingual transfer method for paradigm completion and reports large gains (up to 58% higher accuracy) using labeled data from a high-resource language. Strong empirical evaluation across 21 language pairs in four families (including experiments on SIGMORPHON data, Portuguese–Spanish and Arabic–Spanish) suggests the results will change how researchers approach morphology transfer and data-efficient learning. The work also clearly identifies limits—transfer succeeds mainly among related languages and unrelated-language gains can reflect regularization (ciphering experiments)—which both grounds expectations and opens concrete follow-up research directions. Given the empirical gains and broad applicability to low-resource settings, this is a strong-impact contribution likely to be built upon, though not fully transformative across all language scenarios.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—paradigm completion for low-resource languages—by demonstrating one-shot and zero-shot neural cross-lingual transfer and reporting up to 58% accuracy gains using labeled data from high-resource languages. Experimental breadth (21 language pairs across four families, SIGMORPHON 2016 and language-specific experiments) and the clear finding that relatedness strongly conditions transfer make this likely to change how researchers approach low-resource morphology. The limitation that transfer is mainly effective among related languages (and that some gains from unrelated sources may be regularization artifacts) prevents a fully transformative claim, but the work nonetheless opens significant, widely useful research directions for cross-lingual morphology and low-resource NLP.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important low-resource NLP problem by proposing a one-shot/zero-shot neural cross-lingual transfer method for paradigm completion and reports large gains (up to 58% higher accuracy) using labeled data from a high-resource language. Strong empirical evaluation across 21 language pairs in four families (including experiments on SIGMORPHON data, Portuguese–Spanish and Arabic–Spanish) suggests the results will change how researchers approach morphology transfer and data-efficient learning. The work also clearly identifies limits—transfer succeeds mainly among related languages and unrelated-language gains can reflect regularization (ciphering experiments)—which both grounds expectations and opens concrete follow-up research directions. Given the empirical gains and broad applicability to low-resource settings, this is a strong-impact contribution likely to be built upon, though not fully transformative across all language scenarios.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper includes direct comparisons to a monolingual no-transfer baseline and an Arabic baseline on shared datasets (SIGMORPHON 2016, Portuguese–Spanish, Arabic–Spanish) using the same accuracy metric, and it analyzes differences (e.g., language relatedness effects and ciphering experiments showing regularization-driven gains). It also reports hyperparameters and discusses limitations, indicating generally fair and thorough comparisons. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to other recent neural cross-lingual/transfer baselines, a minor omission. | B(4): The paper evaluates its method against a no-transfer (monolingual) baseline and includes control experiments (e.g., ciphering, Arabic source) across 21 language pairs, and it explicitly analyzes when gains are due to linguistic transfer versus regularization. It also acknowledges limitations (transfer mainly among related languages) and reports thorough related-work coverage. However, only monolingual/no-transfer and an Arabic baseline are mentioned in the summary—there is no explicit mention of comparisons to other recent state-of-the-art cross-lingual or morphological-transfer models, so some relevant baselines may be missing.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper includes direct comparisons to a monolingual no-transfer baseline and an Arabic baseline on shared datasets (SIGMORPHON 2016, Portuguese–Spanish, Arabic–Spanish) using the same accuracy metric, and it analyzes differences (e.g., language relatedness effects and ciphering experiments showing regularization-driven gains). It also reports hyperparameters and discusses limitations, indicating generally fair and thorough comparisons. However, the summary does not indicate comparisons to other recent neural cross-lingual/transfer baselines, a minor omission.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates its method against a no-transfer (monolingual) baseline and includes control experiments (e.g., ciphering, Arabic source) across 21 language pairs, and it explicitly analyzes when gains are due to linguistic transfer versus regularization. It also acknowledges limitations (transfer mainly among related languages) and reports thorough related-work coverage. However, only monolingual/no-transfer and an Arabic baseline are mentioned in the summary—there is no explicit mention of comparisons to other recent state-of-the-art cross-lingual or morphological-transfer models, so some relevant baselines may be missing.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experimental suite is broadly convincing: results span 21 language pairs across four families (including SIGMORPHON 2016, Portuguese–Spanish and Arabic–Spanish experiments) and claim up to 58% accuracy gains as well as zero-/one-shot settings. Appropriate baselines and targeted analyses are present — a no-transfer baseline, ciphering experiments to disentangle regularization from true linguistic transfer, and reported hyperparameters — indicating reasonable ablation/sensitivity checks. The paper also discusses failure modes and limitations (transfer mainly among related languages; regularization difficulties). One gap is potential reproducibility: code availability is not mentioned, which would have strengthened completeness. | B(4): The experiments are broad and seem well targeted: evaluation on 21 language pairs across four families, including zero-/one-shot settings and comparisons to a no-transfer baseline and unrelated-source (Arabic) transfer, provides strong empirical support for the claims. The paper includes useful sensitivity checks (e.g., ciphering experiments to separate true linguistic transfer from regularization effects) and reports hyperparameters, but the summary does not indicate systematic ablations of model components or extensive sensitivity sweeps. Related work is described as thorough, limitations (transfer limited to related languages; regularization challenges) are discussed, and reporting of hyperparameters makes reproduction plausible, though absence of mentioned code weakens complete reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental suite is broadly convincing: results span 21 language pairs across four families (including SIGMORPHON 2016, Portuguese–Spanish and Arabic–Spanish experiments) and claim up to 58% accuracy gains as well as zero-/one-shot settings. Appropriate baselines and targeted analyses are present — a no-transfer baseline, ciphering experiments to disentangle regularization from true linguistic transfer, and reported hyperparameters — indicating reasonable ablation/sensitivity checks. The paper also discusses failure modes and limitations (transfer mainly among related languages; regularization difficulties). One gap is potential reproducibility: code availability is not mentioned, which would have strengthened completeness.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are broad and seem well targeted: evaluation on 21 language pairs across four families, including zero-/one-shot settings and comparisons to a no-transfer baseline and unrelated-source (Arabic) transfer, provides strong empirical support for the claims. The paper includes useful sensitivity checks (e.g., ciphering experiments to separate true linguistic transfer from regularization effects) and reports hyperparameters, but the summary does not indicate systematic ablations of model components or extensive sensitivity sweeps. Related work is described as thorough, limitations (transfer limited to related languages; regularization challenges) are discussed, and reporting of hyperparameters makes reproduction plausible, though absence of mentioned code weakens complete reproducibility.

main_claims: A novel cross-lingual transfer method for paradigm completion using a neural encoder-decoder model is presented.

Using labeled data from a high-resource language improves performance on low-resource languages, achieving up to 58% higher accuracy than without transfer in experiments on 21 language pairs across four language families.
The method enables zero-shot and one-shot learning for morphological paradigm completion.
The degree of language relatedness strongly influences the ability to transfer morphological knowledge.
_summary.methodology: Joint multi-task training that ties parameters across a high-resource and a low-resource language, parameterizing each conditional distribution with an encoder-decoder gated RNN with attention; inputs are concatenations of a language tag, the morphological tag, and the characters of the lemma.
limitations: Transfer is viable primarily among related languages (transfer between unrelated languages is unsuccessful).
In low-resource settings training required attention to regularization (regularization was a noted difficulty).
For unrelated source languages (e.g., Arabic) improvements can stem from regularization effects rather than true linguistic transfer (ciphering experiments showed disrupted true transfer).
